Santa Clara University · HCI & Privacy Design

Click Cleverly:
Digital Consent, Redesigned

AdvisorDr. Kai Lukoff
TeamAarush Malik, Siddhi Kabadi, Sanaa Ahmed
CourseCOEN 129 · HCI Capstone
MethodsInterviews · Figma · Usability Testing

91% of people agree to Terms of Service without reading them. Click Cleverly transforms mindless clicking into informed decision-making: using interactive quizzes, color-coded risk flags, and plain-language summaries to put users back in control of their data.

91%Don't read TOS
97%18–34 year olds

01

The Problem

Terms of Service agreements are often ignored due to their long, dense and wordy language. As a result, they fail to deliver important information regarding data collection, content ownership, and user rights.

This exceeds being a generic usability problem. It's a consent problem. When 91% of users click "I agree" without reading, and companies know this, digital privacy and consent can be exploited, and businesses can infringe privacy.

Click Cleverly asks: what if the interface worked for users instead of against them?

"The internet should work for users, not against them."
02

Our Solution

Fig 1: Click Cleverly Brand Identity

The Click Cleverly identity: a browser extension and mobile tool for transparent, honest digital consent.

Click Cleverly provides instant, digestible summaries of Terms of Service and privacy policies at the moment of decision-making. Instead of forcing users to go through legal jargon, it summarizes critical details into plain language, and then makes users engage with them through an interactive quiz before they can agree.

Three core features drive the system: a plain-language quiz that reframes legal clauses as personal questions, a color-coded risk scale that states concerning clauses visually, and alternative service suggestions for when users decide a TOS is unacceptable.

03

Core Features

Interactive Consent Quiz

Legal clauses translated into direct yes/no questions: "Do you agree to let this company sell your data?" Forces active engagement rather than passive skimming.

Color-Coded Risk Flags

A red/yellow/green risk scale visually highlights concerning clauses in the contract itself. Counters dark patterns that make "Agree" more salient than "Decline."

Personalized Summaries

Users declare how they're using an app (personal vs. business) and receive a customized summary highlighting the risks most relevant to them.

Safer Alternatives

If a TOS is flagged as high-risk, users receive recommendations for similar services with more user-friendly privacy policies.

04

Tech Stack & Methods

Figma Semi-Structured Interviews Behavioral Logging Heuristic Evaluation Usability Testing Iterative Prototyping Privacy Policy Analysis Dark Pattern Research
05

Design Walkthrough

Two core interaction flows: an interactive consent quiz that translates legal jargon into plain questions, and a color-coded risk scale that visually flags concerning clauses in real contracts.

Read on Medium ↗
01

Feature 1 — Interactive Consent Quiz

Before a user can accept a Terms of Service agreement, Click Cleverly presents a short yes/no quiz that translates complex legal clauses into direct, human-readable questions. Instead of passive skimming, users actively engage with the implications of what they're agreeing to.

Example questions the quiz uses instead of legal text:

  • Q1"Do you agree to let this company sell your data to third parties?"
  • Q2"Are you okay with losing the right to sue this company in court?"
  • Q3"Do you agree that this service can track your location at all times?"
Fig 2 — Interactive Quiz Flow (Motel Rocks shopping site scenario)

From unanswered questions → user selecting answers → personalized outcome: "Allowing this app to track you may not be the safest choice." The quiz surfaces consequences in real time.

Fig 3 — Post-Quiz: Safer Alternatives & Preference Saved

After rejecting a TOS, users receive recommendations for similar services with better privacy protections — so "no" isn't a dead end.

02

Feature 2 — Color-Coded Risk Highlighting

Click Cleverly overlays the actual TOS text with color-coded highlights and explanations, so users can instantly spot which clauses deserve attention. The system categorizes risk using a three-level scale:

High
"This company can sell your personal data to third parties." The clause explicitly removes legal protections you might otherwise have.
Medium
"Your data may be used for targeted advertising." — There's a privacy breach as third parties can see the kind of content you interact with.
Low
"This service uses cookies to improve user experience." Standard industry practice
Fig 4 — Risk Highlighting in Action (Hinge dating app TOS)

The same clause is flagged differently based on its risk category. Orange (medium): Hinge can send DMCA notices on your behalf but has no obligation. Red (high): Hinge is not obligated to protect your content. Green (low): standard protective consumer clause.

Fig 5 — Quick Summary & Quiz Integration

After clicking through individual risk flags, a plain-language "Quick Overview" summarizes the key implications, and then flows directly into the consent quiz.

03

Feature 3 — Personalized Preference Onboarding

Users are not equally affected by every clause. A privacy-conscious user cares more about location tracking than content licensing; a business user might prioritize data ownership differently. Click Cleverly's onboarding lets users declare their preferences upfront so summaries are tailored to what matters most to them.

Fig 6 — Preference Setup Flow

Four-step onboarding establishes the user's personal thresholds for data collection, location access, and third-party sharing, personalizing every summary that follows.

siddhi kabadi · santa clara university HCI · Privacy · Digital Consent

Santa Clara University · HCI Capstone

Research & Process

User research through semi-structured interviews and behavioral logging: followed by iterative prototyping, heuristic evaluation, and usability testing that shaped every design decision.

01

Design Process

Phase 01

User Research

Semi-structured interviews with 5 participants (including a J.D. candidate and a CS undergraduate) + behavioral logging over one week.

Phase 02

Paper Prototyping

Sketched interaction flows on paper and iPad, testing how to structure TOS summaries before committing to digital mockups.

Phase 03

Figma Prototyping

Digital mockups in Figma with iterative heuristic evaluation and usability testing with peers across multiple rounds.

02

Early Design Sketches

Before building in Figma, we sketched the core interaction model on paper. The earliest concept, a clickable popup overlay on a live TOS page, established the core UX principle: user-initiated, not intrusive. "User interactive, user chooses if they want to see it."

Fig 7 — Initial Paper Prototype (Design Idea #1)

The earliest sketch: a TOS page with a "Click Cleverly" popup overlay listing what to look out for and legal actions. Designed for apps with high-stakes privacy implications (medical, banking). Notes: "Clickable to show popup. User interactive, user chooses if they want to see it."

03

Key Research Findings

Finding 01: From Interviews

Legal Jargon is a Major Barrier; Even for Experts

Even participants with legal training found themselves glossing over dense TOS clauses.

"Even as a law student, I find myself stressing over legal clauses because they're unnecessarily long and confusing."
Finding 02: From Behavioral Logs

Consent Has Become Invisible

Participants logged only 2–5 TOS interactions per day, despite visiting an average of 130 web pages daily. The behavioral logs revealed that most consent moments go completely unregistered. TOS agreements have become background noise, encountered so frequently that users have learned to treat them as obstacles rather than decisions.

This finding drove the decision to make Click Cleverly a pop-up at the moment of decision — surfacing consent when it matters rather than requiring users to seek it out.

Finding 03: From Behavioral Logs

Interface Design Shapes Consent Behavior

The visual design of TOS interfaces: bright green "Agree" buttons, gray "Decline" links: systematically steers users toward acceptance. This is a documented dark pattern: making one choice visually dominant is a form of manipulation.

"The bright green 'Agree' button makes it tempting to just click and move on."

Click Cleverly's response: redesign the interface so visual emphasis supports informed choice, not passive acceptance. The quiz creates design friction, an intentional pause before agreement.

04

Insights from Iterative Testing

Usability Insight 01

Passive Warnings Don't Work

During early testing, users dismissed risk highlights immediately without reading them. The warnings looked optional, so users treated them as optional. The fix: a forced acknowledgment system requiring users to click "Confirm" before proceeding past high-risk clauses.

Usability Insight 02

Not All Risks Are Equal; Personalization Matters

Privacy-conscious users cared deeply about location tracking but shrugged at content licensing. Business users had nearly the opposite priorities. Treating all risks as equally urgent produced alert fatigue. The fix: a personalized preference system that weights summaries by what each user actually cares about.

Usability Insight 03

Friendly Alternatives

After going through the quiz and deciding a TOS was unacceptable, users felt stuck. Without alternatives, they felt pressured to accept terms they'd just chosen to reject. The fix: the alternative service recommender, so declining is a real option rather than a dead end.

05

Conclusion

Click Cleverly demonstrates that the consent problem is a design problem. Addressing this requires intervening at the interface level: making risk legible, making agreement active, and making refusal a TOS feasible.

The project also surfaces a deeper question about who controls the design of consent infrastructure. When platforms control both the terms and the interface for accepting them, users have no autonomy. Tools like Click Cleverly allow users to make informed desicions regarding their privacy.
